description: Curated structural sequences, disorder annotations, and disease associations for 12 neurodegenerative disease targets spanning Alzheimer's, Parkinson's, Huntington's, ALS, and prion diseases.
author: James Paul Trageser

targets.name: Alpha-synuclein
targets.disease: Parkinson's disease, Dementia with Lewy bodies
targets.pathogenic_mechanism: Alpha-synuclein is natively unfolded but undergoes pathological aggregation into amyloid fibrils that form Lewy bodies. The A53T, A30P, and E46K familial mutations accelerate fibril nucleation. Oligomeric intermediates are believed to be the primary toxic species, disrupting mitochondrial membranes and synaptic vesicle trafficking.
targets.therapeutic_relevance: Aggregation inhibitors, immunotherapy (prasinezumab), and small molecules targeting the NAC region (residues 61-95) are in clinical trials.
targets.key_residues: NAC region (61-95): aggregation-prone core; N-terminal repeats (1-60): membrane binding; C-terminal acidic tail (96-140): chaperone interactions

targets.name: Tau (longest isoform, 2N4R)
targets.disease: Alzheimer's disease, frontotemporal dementia, progressive supranuclear palsy
targets.pathogenic_mechanism: Tau is a microtubule-associated protein that becomes hyperphosphorylated in disease, detaching from microtubules and self-assembling into paired helical filaments (PHFs) and neurofibrillary tangles (NFTs). Cryo-EM structures reveal disease-specific fibril polymorphs. The P301L mutation dramatically accelerates aggregation.
targets.therapeutic_relevance: Tau immunotherapy (semorinemab, zagotenemab), aggregation inhibitors (methylene blue derivatives), ASOs reducing tau expression (BIIB080/ISIS 814907), and kinase inhibitors targeting GSK-3beta are under investigation.
targets.key_residues: R1-R4 repeat domains (244-368): microtubule binding and aggregation core; PHF6 (306-311, VQIVYK): primary nucleation motif; PHF6* (275-280, VQIINK): secondary nucleation motif

targets.name: Amyloid-beta 42
targets.pathogenic_mechanism: Abeta-42 is a proteolytic fragment of amyloid precursor protein (APP) cleaved by beta- and gamma-secretases. It forms toxic oligomers and amyloid plaques in the brain. The two additional C-terminal residues (I41, A42) compared to Abeta-40 dramatically increase aggregation propensity and hydrophobicity.
targets.therapeutic_relevance: Anti-amyloid antibodies lecanemab (FDA-approved 2023) and donanemab (FDA-approved 2024) target amyloid plaques. BACE1 inhibitors and gamma-secretase modulators aim to reduce Abeta production.
targets.key_residues: Central hydrophobic cluster (17-21, LVFFA): nucleation core; C-terminal residues (29-42): fibril stabilization; His6, His13, His14: metal ion coordination sites implicated in oxidative stress

targets.name: Huntingtin (exon 1, 21Q normal)
targets.pathogenic_mechanism: Huntingtin exon 1 contains a polyglutamine (polyQ) tract that expands beyond 35 repeats in Huntington's disease. Expanded polyQ forms beta-sheet-rich amyloid fibrils and nuclear inclusions. The flanking proline-rich domain and N17 amphipathic helix modulate aggregation kinetics. Toxic gain-of-function through transcriptional dysregulation, mitochondrial impairment, and proteostasis collapse.
targets.therapeutic_relevance: HTT-lowering ASOs (tominersen), allele-selective siRNAs, and splice-modulating compounds are in development. Small molecule aggregation inhibitors and PROTAC-mediated degradation strategies are also under investigation.
targets.key_residues: N17 (1-17): amphipathic helix, membrane anchoring; PolyQ tract (18-38 in normal): aggregation trigger when expanded; Proline-rich domain (39-97): aggregation modulator

targets.name: TDP-43
targets.disease: ALS (~97% of cases), frontotemporal dementia (~50% of cases)
targets.pathogenic_mechanism: TDP-43 is an RNA-binding protein that undergoes nuclear-to-cytoplasmic mislocalization, hyperphosphorylation, ubiquitination, and C-terminal cleavage in disease. The low-complexity domain (LCD, residues 274-414) drives liquid-liquid phase separation (LLPS) and aggregation. Disease involves both loss of nuclear RNA splicing function (cryptic exon inclusion in STMN2, UNC13A) and toxic cytoplasmic gain-of-function. Cryo-EM reveals polymorphic amyloid fibrils with distinct 'dagger' and 'R-shaped' folds.
targets.therapeutic_relevance: ASOs restoring STMN2 and UNC13A splicing; small molecules preventing LLPS-to-solid transition; autophagy enhancers promoting aggregate clearance.
targets.key_residues: RRM1 (106-176) and RRM2 (191-259): RNA recognition motifs; LCD/prion-like domain (274-414): aggregation-prone region; Gly-rich region (274-313): early aggregation nucleation

targets.name: FUS
targets.pathogenic_mechanism: FUS contains an N-terminal prion-like low-complexity domain (LCD) enriched in QGSY residues that undergoes LLPS. NLS mutations (e.g., R521G, P525L) cause cytoplasmic mislocalization and irreversible aggregation. Like TDP-43, pathology involves both loss of nuclear RNA processing function and toxic cytoplasmic inclusions. RNA plays a dual role, solubilizing FUS at physiological ratios but promoting aggregation when RNA homeostasis is disrupted.
targets.therapeutic_relevance: FUS-targeted ASOs; compounds modulating phase separation dynamics; nuclear import enhancers.
targets.key_residues: QGSY-rich LCD (1-165): phase separation domain; RGG domains: RNA binding and phase separation modulation; RRM (285-370): RNA recognition; ZnF (422-453): DNA/RNA binding; NLS (514-526): nuclear localization, mutation hotspot

targets.name: Prion protein (PrP)
targets.disease: Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease, fatal familial insomnia, Gerstmann-Straussler-Scheinker syndrome, kuru
targets.pathogenic_mechanism: PrP undergoes a conformational switch from alpha-helix-rich PrP-C to beta-sheet-rich PrP-Sc, which is self-templating and infectious. The octapeptide repeat region (51-91) and hydrophobic domain (112-135) are critical for conversion. PrP-Sc forms amyloid fibrils resistant to protease digestion.
targets.therapeutic_relevance: Anti-prion antibodies; compounds stabilizing PrP-C conformation; GPI-anchor inhibitors reducing cell-surface PrP.
targets.key_residues: Octapeptide repeats (51-91): copper binding, expansion causes familial CJD; Hydrophobic domain (112-135): conversion initiation; GPI anchor signal (231-253)

targets.name: Amylin (IAPP)
targets.pathogenic_mechanism: IAPP is co-secreted with insulin from pancreatic beta cells. The 20-29 region is highly amyloidogenic and forms fibrils that deposit as islet amyloid, contributing to beta cell death and insulin secretion impairment. Cross-seeding between IAPP and Abeta aggregates has been reported, linking T2DM and AD risk.
targets.therapeutic_relevance: Pramlintide (non-aggregating IAPP analog) is FDA-approved for diabetes management. IAPP aggregation inhibitors are under investigation.
targets.key_residues: Amyloidogenic region (20-29, SNNFGAILSS): primary aggregation core; Disulfide bond (C2-C7): constrains N-terminal loop

targets.name: CREB1
targets.disease: Cognitive decline in neurodegeneration (memory consolidation pathway)
targets.pathogenic_mechanism: CREB1 is a transcription factor critical for long-term memory formation and neuronal survival. CREB-dependent gene expression is impaired in Alzheimer's and Huntington's disease models. Abeta oligomers suppress CREB phosphorylation at Ser133, disrupting synaptic plasticity and BDNF expression.
targets.therapeutic_relevance: CREB pathway activators (e.g., rolipram, PDE4 inhibitors) enhance memory in preclinical AD models. CREB-binding protein (CBP) is a therapeutic target for restoring histone acetylation in polyQ diseases.
targets.key_residues: KID domain (100-160): kinase-inducible domain, Ser133 phosphorylation site; bZIP domain (280-341): DNA binding and dimerization

targets.name: PINK1
targets.pathogenic_mechanism: PINK1 is a mitochondrial serine/threonine kinase that acts as a sensor for mitochondrial damage. On depolarized mitochondria, PINK1 accumulates on the outer membrane and phosphorylates ubiquitin (Ser65) and Parkin, initiating mitophagy. Loss-of-function mutations cause accumulation of damaged mitochondria, oxidative stress, and dopaminergic neuron death. The first near-atomic structure of PINK1 bound to the TOM complex was published in 2025.
targets.therapeutic_relevance: Mitophagy enhancers (MTK458, FB231); small molecules that activate PINK1 kinase activity or bypass PINK1 loss; kinetin triphosphate (neo-substrates).
targets.key_residues: Mitochondrial targeting sequence (1-34); Transmembrane domain (94-110); Kinase domain (156-509): catalytic activity; Autophosphorylation sites (Ser228, Ser402)

targets.name: Parkin
targets.pathogenic_mechanism: Parkin is an E3 ubiquitin ligase that is recruited to depolarized mitochondria by PINK1-phosphorylated ubiquitin. Once activated by PINK1 phosphorylation at Ser65 of its ubiquitin-like (Ubl) domain, Parkin ubiquitinates outer mitochondrial membrane proteins, flagging damaged mitochondria for autophagic degradation. Over 200 pathogenic mutations cause loss of E3 ligase activity.
targets.therapeutic_relevance: Gene therapy delivering functional PRKN; small molecules that release Parkin autoinhibition; compounds enhancing mitophagy downstream of Parkin.
targets.key_residues: Ubl domain (1-76): Ser65 phosphorylation by PINK1; RING0 (141-225): autoinhibitory element; RING1 (238-293): E2 binding; IBR (328-377); RING2 (418-449): catalytic Cys431

targets.name: LRRK2 (kinase-GTPase fragment)
targets.disease: Autosomal dominant Parkinson's disease (PARK8), most common genetic cause of PD
targets.pathogenic_mechanism: LRRK2 is a large multidomain kinase whose G2019S mutation (in the kinase domain) is the most common genetic cause of Parkinson's disease. Hyperactive LRRK2 kinase phosphorylates Rab GTPases (Rab8a, Rab10, Rab12), disrupting endolysosomal trafficking, ciliogenesis, and mitophagy. LRRK2 associates with microtubules via its WD40 domain, and pathogenic mutations enhance this association.
targets.therapeutic_relevance: LRRK2 kinase inhibitors (DNL151/BIIB122, MLi-2) are in clinical trials. Alternative strategies include antisense oligonucleotides reducing LRRK2 expression and disruption of LRRK2 dimerization.
targets.key_residues: ROC GTPase domain: R1441C/G/H mutations; COR-B domain: Y1699C mutation; Kinase domain: G2019S (activation loop), I2020T; WD40: microtubule association
